cards.back: An ambiguity that occurs when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from the same base class, resulting in duplicate base class subobjects

cards.text: To make a base class virtual, use the {{c1::virtual}} keyword in the inheritance specification: class Derived: {{c1::virtual}} public Base

cards.front: What problem do virtual base classes solve?
cards.back: They solve the diamond problem by ensuring only one copy of the base class exists in a derived object, even when inherited through multiple paths.

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.answer: Two PoweredDevice subobjects - one from Scanner and one from Printer.
cards.explanation: Without virtual inheritance, Copier has two separate copies of PoweredDevice, which wastes memory and creates ambiguity when accessing PoweredDevice members.

cards.text: With virtual base classes: class Scanner: {{c1::virtual public}} PoweredDevice \{ \};

cards.question: How many PoweredDevice subobjects does c have with virtual inheritance?
cards.answer: One PoweredDevice subobject, shared between Scanner and Printer paths.
cards.explanation: Virtual inheritance ensures only a single shared base class subobject exists, solving the diamond problem.

cards.front: Who is responsible for constructing a virtual base class?
cards.back:
The most-derived class is responsible for constructing the virtual base class directly, not the intermediate classes.

Example: In Copier: Scanner: Printer: PoweredDevice, Copier constructs PoweredDevice.

cards.text: With virtual base classes, the {{c1::most-derived}} class must call the virtual base class constructor in its initialization list.

cards.explanation:
Virtual base classes have several costs:
  • More complex construction rules (most-derived class must initialize)
  • Performance overhead (extra indirection to access members)
  • Increased object size (extra pointers for virtual base tracking)
  • More complicated memory layout
Use only when necessary to solve the diamond problem.
examples: // With virtual inheritance: solves diamond, but slower

cards.front: When should you use virtual base classes?
cards.back:
Only when you have a diamond inheritance pattern and need a single shared base class subobject. Avoid if possible due to complexity and performance costs.

Consider composition or redesign instead.

cards.question: What's required to make this code compile?
cards.answer: Derived must call Base constructor directly in its initialization list.
cards.explanation:
With virtual base classes, the most-derived class (Derived) is responsible for initializing the virtual base (Base), even though it's not a direct parent.

Example: Derived(): Base(value), A(), B() { }
